meta.name: Naan (Stovetop Skillet + Oven Broil Finish)
meta.subtitle: Soft, blistered flatbread using yogurt + yeast; no tandoor required

source.name: SSA canonical cuisines/indian/recipes/naan.json
notes: Deterministic home-kitchen naan method designed for cast iron or heavy skillet.
Includes optional oven broil finish to mimic tandoor blistering.
Supports make-ahead dough, freezing, and garlic-butter finishing.

summary: Soft, chewy, blistered naan you can make at home without a tandoor. Yogurt enriches the dough; a hot skillet creates char spots, and a quick broil adds blistering.

optional.label: Stand mixer
optional.notes: Makes kneading easier, but hand-kneading works well.

signals.freezerFriendly: yes (freeze cooked naan, reheat with steam/heat)
signals.weeknightPossible: yes if dough is made ahead

notes: Contains gluten (wheat flour) and dairy (yogurt; butter if used).

ingredients.label: All-purpose flour
notes: Start with 3 cups and reserve 1/4 cup for adjusting. Humidity and yogurt thickness affect dough.
Bread flour yields chewier naan (see variation).

ingredients.label: Instant yeast
notes: Equivalent to 1 packet. If using active dry yeast, bloom it in warm water (see variation).

ingredients.label: Baking powder
ingredients.notes: Classic home-naan helper for lift and tenderness.

ingredients.label: Plain yogurt (full-fat preferred)
notes: Thick yogurt (Greek-style) may require a splash more water.
Full-fat improves tenderness and browning.

ingredients.label: Warm water
notes: Target 100–110°F (38–43°C). Too hot can weaken yeast.
Adjust 1–3 tbsp as needed to form a soft, slightly tacky dough.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil
ingredients.notes: Vegetable, avocado, or light olive oil.

ingredients.label: Butter, melted (for finishing)
ingredients.notes: Swap with ghee for Indian flavor; omit for dairy-free (texture will differ).

ingredients.label: Garlic, finely minced (for garlic naan)
ingredients.notes: Mix into melted butter or press onto dough surface before cooking.

steps.title: Mix dry ingredients
instructions: In a large bowl, whisk together flour, instant yeast, sugar, salt, and baking powder.
checks: Salt should be evenly distributed to avoid yeast hot-spots.

steps.title: Add yogurt, water, and oil
instructions: Add yogurt and oil to the bowl.
Pour in warm water gradually while mixing until a shaggy dough forms.
If dry pockets remain, add 1 tbsp water at a time. Dough should be soft and slightly tacky, not stiff.
checks: Soft dough = tender naan. Stiff dough = dense bread.
Avoid adding too much flour early; hydrate first.

steps.title: Knead until smooth and elastic
instructions: Turn dough onto a lightly floured surface.
Knead 6–8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Dough may remain slightly tacky.
If dough sticks heavily, dust with small amounts of the reserved flour.
checks: Dough should spring back slowly when pressed.
Over-flouring here reduces blistering and tenderness.

steps.title: First rise
instructions: Lightly oil a bowl and place dough inside, turning once to coat.
Cover with a towel or plastic wrap.
Let rise until doubled, 60–75 minutes in a warm spot.
checks: If your kitchen is cool, rise may take up to 90 minutes.
Dough is ready when a finger dent springs back slowly.

steps.title: Portion and rest
instructions: Punch down gently to deflate.
Divide into 6 equal pieces.
Roll into balls, cover, and rest 10 minutes to relax gluten.
checks: Resting makes shaping easier and prevents snap-back.

steps.title: Preheat skillet (very hot)
instructions: Place a cast iron (or heavy) skillet over medium-high heat.
Let it preheat 3–5 minutes until very hot (a drop of water should sizzle and dance).
checks: Hot pan is key to bubbles and char spots.
Avoid adding oil to the pan; naan should dry-cook on the surface.

steps.title: Shape naan
instructions: Working one piece at a time, flatten and stretch/roll into an oval about 1/8–1/4 inch thick.
If desired: lightly dampen one side with water (helps bubbles) and/or press minced garlic onto the top surface.
checks: Thin dough creates more blistering; thicker dough is softer but less charred.
Keep remaining dough covered while shaping to prevent drying.

steps.title: Cook first side (bubble stage)
instructions: Place shaped naan onto the hot skillet (damp side down if you used water).
Cook 45–75 seconds until bubbles form and the underside shows brown spots.
checks: If no bubbles appear, pan may be too cool.
If it scorches instantly, reduce heat slightly.

steps.title: Flip and finish second side
instructions: Flip naan and cook 45–60 seconds until the second side has brown spots and the bread is cooked through.
Optional: For more char, hold naan with tongs and briefly kiss edges to the skillet.
checks: Finished naan should have visible blistering and flexible softness.
If center seems doughy, reduce thickness next round or extend cook by 15–20 seconds.

steps.title: Optional broil finish (tandoor-like blistering)
instructions: Optional: Place cooked naan on a baking sheet 4–6 inches under a hot broiler for 15–30 seconds until blistered.
Watch closely—broilers brown fast.
checks: This step boosts char spots and puffing, closest to tandoor results.

steps.title: Brush and keep warm
instructions: Brush hot naan with melted butter or ghee (optional).
Sprinkle with chopped cilantro if desired.
Stack in a towel-lined basket to stay soft while cooking remaining pieces.
checks: Covering traps steam and keeps naan pliable.
Brush immediately while hot so fat absorbs.

notes: Naan pairs best with saucy curries and dals for scooping.
Also works as a wrap base for kebabs and grilled meats.
Cool fully, store airtight.
Reheat: sprinkle lightly with water and warm in a covered skillet on low, or microwave covered with a damp paper towel.
Freeze cooked naan with parchment between pieces in a freezer bag.
Reheat from frozen: wrap in foil and warm at 350°F for ~10 minutes, or toast in a skillet with a lid.
Ensure naan is cooked through (no raw dough) before storing.
Cool within 2 hours to reduce moisture-related spoilage.
Warm the water to 105–110°F. Stir in sugar and active dry yeast; bloom 5–10 minutes until foamy.
Then add bloomed yeast-water to the dough and proceed as written.
Replace all-purpose flour with bread flour by weight (same volume can vary).
Dough may need 1–2 tbsp extra water.
Cook second side slightly longer (60–90 seconds) and lightly char edges with tongs.
Keep skillet very hot between breads; let it reheat 30–45 seconds if needed.
Mix minced garlic into melted butter/ghee and brush immediately after cooking.
Or press minced garlic onto the top surface before cooking.
After first rise, cover dough and refrigerate 8–24 hours.
Bring to room temperature 45–60 minutes before portioning and shaping.
Cold ferment improves flavor and spotting.
Replace yogurt with unsweetened thick plant yogurt; replace butter/ghee with oil or plant butter.
Expect slightly less browning; broiler finish helps.
Best weeknight flow: mix dough the night before, cook fresh naan in 10–15 minutes.
